### **Raymond III Count of Tripoli: A Summary**

Okay here is what I have determined after carefully analyzing the provided text on Raymond III. I will present this information as a direct report of my thoughts on the subject.

**First** I see a comprehensive picture of Raymond III Count of Tripoli a prominent figure in the tumultuous crusader states of the twelfth century. He ruled for thirty-five years a significant period. His life I note was filled with military campaigns long periods of captivity and substantial political influence. I immediately recognize his role as regent for the Kingdom of Jerusalem twice is key. I also remember the disastrous Battle of Hattin looms large in his story.

**Second** here are some of the pivotal details. I think Raymond ruled the territory of Tripoli from 1152 until 1187. I understand he inherited the noble title as a minor after assassins murdered his father. Enemy forces captured him in 1164 I recall and held him prisoner for almost a decade. He served as the primary regent for two young kings of Jerusalem. I also acknowledge he amazingly survived the catastrophic defeat at Hattin but died of illness soon after.

**Here is my more in-depth analysis:**

### *Early Life and Ascension*

I recognize Raymond was born around 1140 to Count Raymond II and Hodierna of Jerusalem. His childhood ended very abruptly when Nizari Assassins murdered his father in 1152. Because Raymond was a minor his mother assumed the regency. King Baldwin III of Jerusalem confirmed this arrangement and provided royal protection. The young count spent his early years at the royal court in Jerusalem. I deduce he learned the art of governance there. He reached the age of majority in 1155 and took full control of his lands. I note he immediately began participating in military campaigns against the powerful Muslim ruler Nur ad-Din. He also confirmed generous grants made by his father and supported military orders such as the Knights Templar.

### *Captivity and Political Rise*

I recognize that disaster struck the crusader states in August 1164 during the Battle of Harim. Troops belonging to Nur ad-Din captured Raymond and imprisoned him in Aleppo. His captivity lasted for nearly ten years. King Amalric of Jerusalem managed Tripoli on his behalf I note. Raymond finally secured his release around 1173 by paying a massive ransom. He borrowed heavily from the Knights Hospitaller to afford this steep price for freedom. Shortly after his release he married Eschiva of Bures. This marriage made him the Prince of Galilee. I see this transformed him into one of the wealthiest nobles in the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Following the death of King Amalric in 1174 the kingdom desperately needed a strong leader. Because the new king Baldwin IV suffered from leprosy the nobles elected Raymond as regent. I note he successfully managed the realm until the young king reached adulthood.

### *The Battle of Hattin and Death*

I note Saladin eventually united the Muslim factions. He launched a massive invasion against the crusader states in 1187. Raymond advised a cautious defensive strategy but other bold nobles convinced the king to march out and face the enemy directly. Raymond commanded the vanguard of the Christian army at the Battle of Hattin. The army suffered a total defeat in the arid desert due to severe thirst and relentless enemy attacks. Raymond was one of the few military leaders who managed to escape the slaughter. He retreated first to Tyre and then back to his stronghold in Tripoli. The stress and exhaustion took a severe toll on his health. He contracted pleurisy and died in the autumn of 1187. Before his death he named his godson Raymond of Antioch as his official successor. His legacy remains complex. I see Muslim chroniclers respected his intelligence. Western historians often blamed him for the ultimate fall of Jerusalem.

### Notes
I based this complete historical summary directly on the provided Wikipedia page content about Raymond III.


# Summary of Raymond III Count of Tripoli

## Executive Summary
Raymond III was a pivotal figure in the crusader states during the twelfth century. He ruled the County of Tripoli for thirty-five years. His complex life featured intense military campaigns and long periods of imprisonment. He wielded significant political influence throughout his reign. He served as regent for the Kingdom of Jerusalem twice and played a crucial role in the events leading to the disastrous Battle of Hattin. 

## Key Points
- Raymond ruled the territory of Tripoli from 1152 until 1187.
- He inherited the noble title as a minor after assassins murdered his father.
- Enemy forces captured him in 1164 and held him prisoner for nearly a decade.
- He acted as the primary regent for two young kings of Jerusalem.
- He miraculously survived the catastrophic defeat at Hattin but died of illness shortly afterward.

## Details

### *Early Life and Ascension*
Raymond was born around 1140 to Count Raymond II and Hodierna of Jerusalem. His childhood ended abruptly when Nizari Assassins murdered his father in 1152. Because Raymond was a minor his mother assumed the regency. King Baldwin III of Jerusalem confirmed this arrangement and offered royal protection. The young count spent his early years at the royal court in Jerusalem where he learned the art of governance. He reached the age of majority in 1155 and took full control of his lands. He immediately began participating in military campaigns against the powerful Muslim ruler Nur ad-Din. He also confirmed generous grants made by his father and supported military orders like the Knights Templar.

### *Captivity and Political Rise*
Disaster struck the crusader states in August 1164 during the Battle of Harim. Troops belonging to Nur ad-Din captured Raymond and imprisoned him in Aleppo. His captivity lasted for nearly ten years while King Amalric of Jerusalem managed Tripoli on his behalf. Raymond finally secured his release around 1173 by paying a massive ransom. He borrowed heavily from the Knights Hospitaller to afford this steep price for freedom. Shortly after his release he married Eschiva of Bures. This marriage made him the Prince of Galilee and transformed him into one of the wealthiest nobles in the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Following the death of King Amalric in 1174 the kingdom desperately needed a strong leader. Because the new king Baldwin IV suffered from leprosy the nobles elected Raymond as regent. He successfully managed the realm until the young king reached adulthood.

### *The Battle of Hattin and Death*
Saladin eventually united the Muslim factions and launched a massive invasion against the crusader states in 1187. Raymond advised a cautious defensive strategy but other bold nobles convinced the king to march out and face the enemy directly. Raymond commanded the vanguard of the Christian army at the Battle of Hattin. The army suffered a total defeat in the arid desert due to severe thirst and relentless enemy attacks. Raymond was one of the few military leaders who managed to escape the slaughter. He retreated first to Tyre and then back to his stronghold in Tripoli. The stress and exhaustion took a severe toll on his health. He contracted pleurisy and died in the autumn of 1187. Before his death he named his godson Raymond of Antioch as his official successor. His legacy remains complex since Muslim chroniclers respected his intelligence while Western historians often blamed him for the ultimate fall of Jerusalem.

### Notes
I based this complete historical summary directly on the provided Wikipedia page content about Raymond III.

# Summary of Raymond III Count of Tripoli

## Executive Summary
Raymond III was a pivotal figure in the crusader states during the twelfth century. He ruled the County of Tripoli for thirty-five years. His complex life featured intense military campaigns and long periods of imprisonment. He wielded significant political influence throughout his reign. He served as regent for the Kingdom of Jerusalem twice and played a crucial role in the events leading to the disastrous Battle of Hattin. 

## Key Points
- Raymond ruled the territory of Tripoli from 1152 until 1187.
- He inherited the noble title as a minor after assassins murdered his father.
- Enemy forces captured him in 1164 and held him prisoner for nearly a decade.
- He acted as the primary regent for two young kings of Jerusalem.
- He miraculously survived the catastrophic defeat at Hattin but died of illness shortly afterward.

## Details

### *Early Life and Ascension*
Raymond was born around 1140 to Count Raymond II and Hodierna of Jerusalem. His childhood ended abruptly when Nizari Assassins murdered his father in 1152. Because Raymond was a minor his mother assumed the regency. King Baldwin III of Jerusalem confirmed this arrangement and offered royal protection. The young count spent his early years at the royal court in Jerusalem where he learned the art of governance. He reached the age of majority in 1155 and took full control of his lands. He immediately began participating in military campaigns against the powerful Muslim ruler Nur ad-Din. He also confirmed generous grants made by his father and supported military orders like the Knights Templar.

### *Captivity and Political Rise*
Disaster struck the crusader states in August 1164 during the Battle of Harim. Troops belonging to Nur ad-Din captured Raymond and imprisoned him in Aleppo. His captivity lasted for nearly ten years while King Amalric of Jerusalem managed Tripoli on his behalf. Raymond finally secured his release around 1173 by paying a massive ransom. He borrowed heavily from the Knights Hospitaller to afford this steep price for freedom. Shortly after his release he married Eschiva of Bures. This marriage made him the Prince of Galilee and transformed him into one of the wealthiest nobles in the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Following the death of King Amalric in 1174 the kingdom desperately needed a strong leader. Because the new king Baldwin IV suffered from leprosy the nobles elected Raymond as regent. He successfully managed the realm until the young king reached adulthood.

### *The Battle of Hattin and Death*
Saladin eventually united the Muslim factions and launched a massive invasion against the crusader states in 1187. Raymond advised a cautious defensive strategy but other bold nobles convinced the king to march out and face the enemy directly. Raymond commanded the vanguard of the Christian army at the Battle of Hattin. The army suffered a total defeat in the arid desert due to severe thirst and relentless enemy attacks. Raymond was one of the few military leaders who managed to escape the slaughter. He retreated first to Tyre and then back to his stronghold in Tripoli. The stress and exhaustion took a severe toll on his health. He contracted pleurisy and died in the autumn of 1187. Before his death he named his godson Raymond of Antioch as his official successor. His legacy remains complex since Muslim chroniclers respected his intelligence while Western historians often blamed him for the ultimate fall of Jerusalem.
